shoes.name: Brooks Ghost
shoes.note: Reliable daily trainer — soft, forgiving, great for beginners.

shoes.name: ASICS Gel-Nimbus
shoes.note: Extra cushioning if you want maximum impact protection.

shoes.name: Saucony Ride
shoes.note: Balanced feel — not too soft, not too firm. Good all-rounder.

shoes.name: New Balance Fresh Foam 880
shoes.note: Comfortable neutral shoe; wide widths available.

shirts.name: Nike Dri-FIT Miler
shirts.note: Classic beginner top — light, breathable, affordable.

shirts.name: Under Armour Tech 2.0
shirts.note: Soft feel, good value, widely available.

shirts.name: Patagonia Capilene Cool
shirts.note: Great for hot weather and sustainability-minded runners.

shirts.name: Merino base layer (Icebreaker / Smartwool)
shirts.note: Odour-resistant and comfortable for cool-weather runs.

bottoms.name: Nike Challenger Shorts
bottoms.note: Simple 5" inseam with liner — a safe first pick.

bottoms.name: Tracksmith Session Shorts
bottoms.note: Premium feel with a classic look if you want to invest.

bottoms.name: Lululemon Pace Breaker
bottoms.note: Comfortable liner and phone pocket; popular for everyday runs.

bottoms.name: SOAR Running Split Shorts
bottoms.note: Light and fast — better once you're running regularly.

tights.name: Nike Pro Warm Tights
tights.note: Solid cold-weather staple with good warmth-to-weight ratio.

tights.name: Lululemon Fast and Free Tight
tights.note: Multiple pockets and a comfortable high waist.

tights.name: 2XU Compression Tights
tights.note: Snug fit — some runners like the supported feel on long runs.

cap.name: Ciele GOCap
cap.note: The cult favourite — light, stylish, great sweat management.

cap.name: Nike Featherlight Cap
cap.note: Budget-friendly and does the job well.

cap.name: Buff UV Headband (winter alternative)
cap.note: Covers ears in cold weather without overheating your head.

cap.name: Smartwool Merino Beanie
cap.note: For freezing temps when a cap isn't enough.

goggles.name: Goodr
goggles.note: Affordable, fun colours, no-slip fit — perfect starter shades.

goggles.name: Oakley Sutro Lite
goggles.note: Premium option with excellent clarity and stability.

goggles.name: Julbo Fury
goggles.note: Great for trail runners who want wraparound coverage.

sunscreen.name: Neutrogena Sport Face SPF 70
sunscreen.note: Sweat-resistant and less likely to sting eyes than body formulas.

sunscreen.name: Banana Boat Sport Ultra SPF 50
sunscreen.note: Budget-friendly, widely available, solid for arms and legs.

sunscreen.name: Supergoop! Play Everyday Lotion SPF 50
sunscreen.note: Light feel, no white cast — good for daily training.

sunscreen.name: Sun Bum Original SPF 50
sunscreen.note: Popular sport formula with a pleasant scent; reapply on long runs.

chafing-creams.name: Body Glide
chafing-creams.note: The classic stick — apply to thighs, feet, and anywhere that rubs.

chafing-creams.name: Squirrel's Nut Butter
chafing-creams.note: Natural anti-chafe balm; popular with trail runners.

chafing-creams.name: Bandelettes or Nip Guards
chafing-creams.note: Physical barriers for nipple chafing on long runs.

chafing-creams.name: Vaseline
chafing-creams.note: Cheap and works in a pinch — not ideal for fabric stains.

hydration-tablets.name: Nuun Sport
hydration-tablets.note: Light, fizzy, easy on the stomach — great starter electrolyte.

hydration-tablets.name: LMNT
hydration-tablets.note: Higher sodium — popular for heavy sweaters and hot climates.

hydration-tablets.name: Precision Fuel & Hydration
hydration-tablets.note: Customizable sodium levels to match your sweat rate.

hydration-tablets.name: SaltStick FastChews
hydration-tablets.note: Chewable electrolytes if you prefer not to mix drinks.

gels.name: Maurten Gel 100
gels.note: Gentle on the stomach — popular with marathoners.

gels.name: GU Energy Gel
gels.note: Wide flavour range; easy to find at races and shops.

gels.name: Huma Gel
gels.note: Made with chia and fruit — good if standard gels upset your gut.

gels.name: Spring Energy
gels.note: Whole-food style fuel for runners who want real-ingredient options.

hydration-packs.name: Salomon Active Skin 8
hydration-packs.note: Light race vest — popular for half and full marathon training.

hydration-packs.name: Nathan VaporHowe 2.0
hydration-packs.note: Comfortable women's-specific fit with good storage.

hydration-packs.name: Osprey Dyna LT
hydration-packs.note: Hybrid pack/vest — great for trail beginners.

hydration-packs.name: FlipBelt or handheld bottle
hydration-packs.note: Simpler option for shorter long runs — no vest needed yet.

apple-watch.name: Apple Watch SE (2nd gen)
apple-watch.note: Best value — full GPS running features without the premium price.

apple-watch.name: Apple Watch Series 9 / 10
apple-watch.note: Brighter always-on display; easier to read mid-run.

apple-watch.name: Apple Watch Ultra
apple-watch.note: Overkill for beginners — consider only for trail ultras and multi-day battery needs.

garmin.name: Garmin Forerunner 165
garmin.note: Colour AMOLED screen, beginner-friendly — great first Garmin.

garmin.name: Garmin Forerunner 255 / 265
garmin.note: The sweet spot for half and full marathon training.

garmin.name: Garmin Forerunner 55
garmin.note: Cheapest entry point — basic but reliable GPS tracking.

coros.name: Coros Pace 3
coros.note: Best beginner pick — light, accurate GPS, weeks of battery.

coros.name: Coros Pace 2
coros.note: Still solid if you find it discounted — slightly older but capable.

coros.name: Coros Apex 2
coros.note: Step up for trail runners who want maps and sapphire glass.

strava-app.name: Phone-only tracking
strava-app.note: Start here — just open Strava and hit Record before your run.

strava-app.name: Strava + Apple Watch / Garmin / Coros
strava-app.note: Record on your watch; sync automatically when done.

strava-app.name: LetsRunNow dashboard
strava-app.note: Connect Strava in your account for streaks, route comparisons, and HR tips.
